source: Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, Series I (1889); Catena Aurea, tr. Newman (1841). Public domain.

John 8:42-51.text: Beloved, our condition needs much endurance; and endurance is produced when doctrines are deeply rooted. For as no wind is able by its assaults to tear up the oak, which sends down its root into the lower recesses of the earth, so too the soul which is nailed by the fear of God none will be able to overturn. 'If you continue in My word, you are My disciples indeed: and the truth shall make you free.' He said not, 'I will deliver you from bondage,' but allowed them to conjecture. And He promises a great thing, that they should become His disciples.

'Why do you not understand My speech? Because you cannot hear My Word.' You cannot hear, because you will not be corrected; you will not be corrected, because you are the children of the devil by imitation. For the devil made no one, begat no one, created no one; but whosoever imitates the devil, that person, as it were born of him, becomes a child of the devil.

'I came from God and now I am here.' He speaks not of a local change, but of the manner of His existence. For how could He who fills all things come locally? He is said to have come forth from the Father, because He was made flesh, not that He left the bosom of the Father, but appeared visibly in the nature which He assumed.

When He says 'I came forth from God,' He declares His eternal generation — not a coming into being from nothing, but a proceeding from the Father's substance. The Son does not begin to exist; He comes forth eternally, as light from light, as radiance from the sun. His coming into the world does not mark His beginning, but His mission.

He who keeps Christ's word shall never see death — not that the body shall not dissolve, but that the soul, held in the hand of its Creator, passes from death to true life. The death that matters is the second death, eternal separation; from this the faithful are forever delivered through the word they have kept.

John 8:51-59.text: A shameless and a forward thing is wickedness, and when it ought to hide itself, then is it the fiercer. For when they ought to have been pricked by what was said, admiring the boldness and conclusiveness of the words, they even insult Him. Where there was need to instruct them, to pull down their excessive insolence, He was vehement; but when it was needful that He being insulted should bear it, He used much gentleness — thus teaching us to avenge insults offered to God, but to overlook such as are offered to ourselves.

'Your father Abraham rejoiced to see My day; and he saw it, and was glad.' He saw it in spirit, not in the flesh. For He is the Lord of all times, even Abraham's Lord. To the Jews He said, 'Before Abraham was, I AM' — He said not 'I was' but 'I AM,' because as God He knows no past tense. All time is present to Him who is above all time.

'Before Abraham was, I AM.' He does not say 'I was' but 'I AM,' using the present tense to declare His eternal, unchanging existence. This is the Name revealed to Moses at the burning bush: I AM WHO I AM. By this single word He claims for Himself what belongs to God alone — existence without beginning, without change, without end.

They took up stones to cast at Him, but He, hiding Himself, went out of the Temple. Not from fear did He hide — He who had power to lay down His life and take it again — but because the hour of His passion had not yet come. He withdrew, that the stone which the builders rejected might become the cornerstone at the appointed time.

'I AM' is properly used of God alone, whose existence is not derived from another and not bounded by time. All creatures have a 'was' and a 'will be,' but God has only an eternal 'is.' The Son, speaking this word, claims no borrowed dignity but asserts His own essential Godhead — co-eternal with the Father, sharing the one divine nature.

John 6:5-14.text: Let us not contend with violent men, but learn when the doing so brings no hurt to our virtue to give place to their evil counsels. He sat not at any time idly with the disciples, but perhaps carefully conversing with them. Having lifted up His eyes, He beheld the multitudes coming unto Him. He knew which of His disciples needed most instruction; for this is he who afterwards said, 'Show us the Father, and it suffices us,' and on this account Jesus was beforehand bringing him into a proper state.

The five loaves signify the five books of Moses. Rightly are they not wheat loaves but barley; for they belong to the Old Testament. And you know that barley is so formed that we get at the pith of it with difficulty; for the pith is clothed with a covering of husk, and the husk itself is tenacious and closely adhering. Such is the letter of the Old Testament: clothed in a covering of carnal sacraments. But if we get at its pith, it feeds and satisfies.

He ascended the mountain and sat there with His disciples, that by the retired situation He might teach us, whenever we attempt anything great, to withdraw from the tumult of the world. And note that He sat with His disciples before the multitudes came to Him, signifying that we must first be instructed ourselves, before we can teach others.

He does not simply give bread, but breaks it and distributes through the disciples — prefiguring the holy mystery of the Eucharist, in which He breaks His own body for the life of the world. The disciples become ministers of the divine gift, as priests and teachers after Him would distribute to the faithful the bread come down from heaven.

The five loaves increased in the hands of those who broke them, not as something created from nothing, but as something multiplied from what existed — even as the earth in harvest-time renders many grains from a few seeds. This is the work of the same power: He who gives increase to seeds year by year here gives increase to bread in a moment.

John 9:39-10:9.text: They who for the sake of the truth and the confession of Christ suffer anything terrible and are insulted, these are especially honoured. The Jews cast him out from the Temple, and the Lord of the Temple found him; he was separated from that pestilent company, and met with the Fountain of salvation; he was dishonoured by those who dishonoured Christ, and was honoured by the Lord of Angels. Such are the prizes of truth.

'I am the door: by Me if any man enter in, he shall be saved, and shall go in and out, and find pasture.' He shall go in by faith, and go out from faith to sight, from belief to contemplation; and shall find pasture in the eternal refreshment. The pasture of this door is that interior nourishment which the saints find in their hearts when they love God.

There is but one door to the sheepfold, and that door is Christ. All who enter by another way — by philosophy alone, by human tradition alone, by their own righteousness — are thieves and robbers. But he who enters by Christ, confessing Him as Lord, believing Him as Saviour, following Him as Shepherd — that one enters rightly and finds pasture.

The sheep hear His voice, for there is in the soul of every baptized person an instinct for the truth, planted by the Holy Spirit, which recognises the voice of Christ and distinguishes it from the voice of strangers. The hireling speaks for himself; Christ speaks what the Father has given Him. The sheep know the difference not by learning but by grace.

He calls Himself the door, to show that no one can approach the Father except through Him. He calls Himself the shepherd, to show His tender care. And He is both at once — the way in and the guardian within. Through Him we enter; by Him we are kept safe. This is the mystery of the Incarnation: that God became our access and our protection.

John 10:17-28.text: A great matter, beloved, a great matter it is to preside over a Church: a matter needing wisdom and courage as great as that of which Christ speaks, that a man should lay down his life for the sheep, and never leave them deserted or naked; that he should stand against the wolf nobly. For in this the shepherd differs from the hireling; the one always looks to his own safety, caring not for the sheep; the other always seeks that of the sheep, neglecting his own.

'No one takes My life from Me, but I lay it down of Myself.' What great power! What great strength! When other men die, it is from necessity, not from power; the Lord died from power, not from necessity. He had the power to lay down His life and the power to take it up again. This is the shepherd who laid down His life for the sheep — not by compulsion, but by love.

'I know Mine, and Mine know Me.' He speaks not of bare intellectual knowledge, but of a knowledge which implies affection and care, as when He says, 'The Lord knows the way of the righteous.' And so the sheep know their shepherd — not merely recognising Him by sight, but following Him in life, yielding to Him in obedience, hearing His voice with the heart.

The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep — not compelled by any external force, but moved by love alone. He holds His own life in His hand and offers it freely, as a sacrifice both voluntary and saving. In this He differs from every other shepherd: they may risk their lives for the flock, but He alone can lay His life down and take it up again.

The hireling flees because he does not love the sheep, and he does not love them because they are not his own. But Christ says, 'I know Mine and Mine know Me.' This knowing is not mere recognition but intimate communion — the knowledge that a father has of his children, that a bridegroom has of his bride. It is the knowledge of love, tested and proven by sacrifice.

John 10:27-38.text: 'I am the good Shepherd, and know My sheep, and am known of Mine.' He proves one from the other; by giving His mortal life He proves that He gives life immortal. As Paul also says, 'If when we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of His Son, much more being reconciled we shall be saved.'

'I and the Father are one.' Not a solitary one, but one: for the word 'are' distinguishes between the persons, while 'one' declares their unity of nature. They are one, not by confusion of person, but by unity of substance. The Son does not differ from the Father in nature, nor is He another God, but by birth He is what the Father is.

He says, 'I and the Father are one' — one in nature, not one in person. He does not say 'I am the Father,' nor does He say 'I and the Father am one thing.' The plural 'are' distinguishes the persons; the singular 'one' declares the unity of substance. Here is the whole mystery of the Trinity expressed in three words: distinction without division, unity without confusion.

They accuse Him of blasphemy, but He answers from their own Scriptures: 'Is it not written in your law, I said you are gods?' If those who received God's word could be called gods by grace, how much more He whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world? The argument moves from lesser to greater: if Scripture honours mortal judges with the divine name, shall the eternal Son be denied it?

When He says 'the Father is in Me and I in the Father,' He speaks not of a spatial indwelling, as water in a cup, but of an identity of nature and will. The radiance is in the light and the light in the radiance — not by position but by nature. So the Son, being the Father's own Word and Radiance, is ever in the Father and the Father in Him, and they are one God.

John 9:1-38.text: Being full of love for man, and caring for our salvation, and desiring to stop the mouths of the foolish, He omits nothing of His own part. When they would not receive His sublime sayings, but said that He had a devil, He went forth from the Temple, and healed the blind, mitigating their rage by His absence, and by working the miracle softening their hardness and cruelty. 'Neither has this man sinned, nor his parents' — He said not that it is possible to sin from one's very birth, but that the Son of God should be glorified in him.

He spat on the ground and made clay of the spittle. The Word was mingled with earth: that is the meaning of the clay. 'He anointed his eyes.' He was anointed but did not yet see. He sent him to the pool called Siloam. The Evangelist thought it worthwhile to tell us the meaning of the name: 'which is by interpretation, Sent.' You know now who was sent; for unless He had been sent, none of us would have been set free from sin.

That which the Artificer, the Word, had omitted to form in the womb, He supplied in public, that the works of God might be manifested in him. For it was no other than the hand of God which fashioned us at the beginning; and so the same hand formed the sight of the man born blind, showing the hand which had moulded us in secret.

The man born blind is the human race — blind from birth through the sin of our first parents. Christ anoints his eyes with clay, for He who formed man from the earth restores sight by the same element, teaching that He who made us is the same who heals us. He sends him to wash in Siloam, which means 'Sent,' for we are cleansed by the One who was sent from the Father.

The blind man washed and came seeing — a figure of baptism, in which the one who comes in darkness departs enlightened. The pool of Siloam is the font of Christ: He who is sent from the Father sends us to the waters of regeneration, where the clay of our mortality is washed away and we receive the light of faith.

John 4:5-42.text: Christ, wearied with His journey, sat thus on the well. It was the sixth hour. Observe how the Evangelist explains all with exact care. He mentions both the fatigue and the sitting, and the time, to show that the weariness was real. And He who is the Fountain of living water asks for a drink — not because He needed it, but because He was fishing for a soul. For He who sought the lost sheep was not weary of seeking this wandering one.

Jesus, wearied from His journey, sat on the well. His very weariness is His strength at work. For the power of God made all things; His weakness made you. By His power He created us, by His weakness He came to seek us. He nourished the weak with milk, that they might be made strong for solid food. He came as a weak man to a weak woman — but in that weakness He drew her to the font of strength.

'If you knew the gift of God, and who it is that says to you, Give Me to drink, you would have asked of Him, and He would have given you living water.' He leads her gently from earthly things to spiritual, from the visible to the invisible. He does not immediately rebuke her ignorance, but invites her wonder, that through wonder she might be drawn to ask, and through asking she might learn.

The Samaritan woman becomes an image of the whole Gentile world — estranged from the covenants, dwelling outside the inheritance of Israel, yet not refused the living water. Christ sits at the well not by accident but by divine economy, waiting for her whom He foreknew, that the water which springs up to eternal life might be offered freely to all who thirst.

She came to draw water at the sixth hour — that is, at midday — when the heat was greatest. For she who was burdened with sins came at the hour of greatest heat. But the Lord, who came to bear our infirmities, sat wearied at the well, that He who was wearied for our sakes might refresh us with the water of life.

John 8:31-42.text: 'If you continue in My word, you are My disciples indeed.' For many even of those who had believed were not firmly grounded. And therefore He seeks continuance. For it is not enough merely to believe, unless we also endure to the end. 'And you shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free.' He speaks not of ordinary knowledge, but of that which comes through abiding — a living acquaintance, not merely intellectual.

'The truth shall make you free.' Free from what? From sin, from death, from the devil. Why is this freedom necessary? Because everyone who commits sin is a slave of sin. A hard master is sin: it commands wicked things, and wicked things are done. But the Son sets free truly. What the Son gives is true freedom — not freedom to sin, but freedom from sin.

True freedom is not found in the assertion of one's own will, but in liberation from the tyranny of sin. The Jews boasted of their descent from Abraham, but Christ teaches that lineage of the flesh profits nothing without likeness of faith. Abraham rejoiced to see the day of Christ; they who claim his name reject the One whom Abraham adored.

Everyone who commits sin is a slave to sin. How many think themselves free who are bound by countless chains of evil habit! The drunkard, the wrathful, the envious — each is led captive at the devil's will. But the Son makes free indeed: not by loosening chains of iron, but by breaking chains of sin through grace.

He said not merely 'you shall be free,' but 'you shall be free indeed' — pointing to a freedom far higher than political liberty. For this freedom extends not merely to the present life, but is eternal, and delivers not the body from barbarian masters, but the soul from the tyranny of the devil.
